what_it_does: This file is a design argument for dictation correction in a voice-first message composer. It proposes a two-tier strategy: first, send the noisy transcript together with rich context to a Claude-Code member so the LLM recipient can disambiguate; second, when the user does want to fix errors, provide one-handed touch-based repair affordances (tap-a-word-to-respeak, select-and-say commands, inline candidate chips) and reserve keyboard entry as a last resort. It also requires the composer to visually mark low-confidence words.
observations: Cites GestureVoice and VoiceAlign research to justify the correction problem
Uses the author's own transcript errors as concrete evidence that phone dictation cleanup is painful
Proposes adding transcript_raw and transcript_confidence fields to the message envelope
Defines a design rule that low-confidence words must be visibly marked
Treats the receiver as an LLM rather than a database as a first-line correction mechanism
Explicitly positions keyboard input as the floor/last resort, not the default
standouts: The core insight that the receiver is an LLM, so messy transcript + rich context can be the first correction tier
Inline candidate chips identified as the highest-ROI one-handed correction affordance
The design rule requiring visible low-confidence word marking so the user does not have to proofread everything
conventions: Follows the noticeboard item frontmatter schema with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, and history
Uses markdown headings, bold labels, and bullet lists for structured argumentation
Cites external research inline without full bibliographic links
Functions as a design proposal rather than a bug report or implementation ticket
concerns: Assumes an LLM recipient can reliably disambiguate noisy transcripts without validating that claim
Provides no implementation details, acceptance criteria, or platform constraints
Does not define how transcript confidence would be computed or surfaced
Research citations lack concrete links or reproducible references
notes: A design note arguing that voice-first dictation correction should rely first on LLM recipient context and second on one-handed touch repair, not re-dictation or keyboard.
questions: What exact fields constitute the 'full envelope' beyond target content, surrounding context, code/board location, and intent?
How would transcript_confidence be calculated and what threshold triggers low-confidence marking?
Which speech recognizer or platform would supply candidate alternates for inline chips?
What is the parent item-60542ab3 and how does this item relate to it?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the title frames the dictation-correction problem as the deciding factor for voice-first viability, and the file delivers a design argument and concrete affordances for that problem.
intention_signals: It is trying to settle the design direction for dictation correction in a voice-driven composer by leveraging the intelligence of the recipient and minimizing the user's repair effort.
novelty: It treats the recipient being an LLM as a primary correction strategy rather than relying solely on better recognition or traditional correction UI, and it pairs that with one-handed touch affordances like inline candidate chips.
contribution: Contributes a two-tier dictation-correction model, specific UI affordances (tap-a-word-to-respeak, select-and-say-command, inline candidate chips, visible low-confidence markers), and proposed envelope fields (transcript_raw, transcript_confidence) for a voice-to-LLM messaging system.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard design item proposing a two-tier dictation-correction strategy for a voice-first composer that sends noisy transcripts with rich context to an LLM recipient and provides one-handed touch repair affordances with low-confidence word marking.
intention_for_embedding: To define how dictation errors should be handled in a voice-first system by leaning on the LLM recipient and offering minimal-effort touch correction instead of re-dictation or keyboard.
